methods.name: Stage-Progression
methods.rationale: Applies typical Series B → next-stage step-up multiplier (1.9x) to $57M total raised proxy.

methods.name: Peer-Median
methods.rationale: Median across 5 same-sector + same-stage peers in AI & Software. Range: $500M - $32.0B.

public.rationale: Public-co frontier-tech comp in AI & Compute

private.name: Safe Superintelligence
private.rationale: Same sector (AI & Software), same stage (Series B), post-money $32B

private.name: Physical Intelligence
private.rationale: Same sector (AI & Software), same stage (Series B), post-money $5.6B

private.name: Photoroom
private.rationale: Same sector (AI & Software), same stage (Series B), post-money $500M

data.narrative:
Zama is a Series B–stage AI & Software company founded by Rand Hindi and Pascal Paillier that has raised $57M+ to date. The valuation assessment draws on a peer universe of five same-stage, same-sector private companies—Safe Superintelligence ($32.0B), Physical Intelligence ($5.6B), World Labs ($5.0B), Nominal ($1.0B), and Photoroom ($500M)—alongside one public comparable (Palantir). This peer set spans a wide range, from $500M to $32.0B, reflecting material heterogeneity within frontier AI.

Two valuation methods were applied. Stage-progression analysis, anchoring to total capital raised as a proxy baseline, applies a 1.9x step-up multiplier typical of Series B progression, yielding $108M. Peer-median analysis takes the median valuation across the five private comparables, producing $5.0B. The wide spread between these methods reflects the outsized valuations commanded by leading frontier-AI firms in the current market.

Triangulated analysis yields a range of $92M (low) to $5.8B (high), with a median of $2.6B. The low end reflects stage-progression logic; the high end anchors to peer medians. Critical uncertainties include Zama's specific competitive positioning within the frontier-AI stack, capital efficiency relative to peers, and path to material revenue. The wide valuation dispersion across the peer set itself—spanning four orders of magnitude—underscores that stage and sector alone provide limited precision in frontier-tech valuation.
